This document details the "Materials in the Automobile" project, a collaborative effort between the U.S. Department of Transportation and the Chrysler Corporation. It outlines a comprehensive assessment of automotive materials, focusing on steel, aluminum, and plastics. The goal is to identify and quantify material improvements to enhance vehicle safety, fuel efficiency, and overall performance. The project involves analyzing material properties, testing durability, and recommending design changes. It aims to provide data-driven insights for future vehicle development, contributing to safer and more sustainable transportation.

Richard C. Carter; John M. Machey

The project, known as 'Project Phoenix,' was initiated in 1975 by the Department of Defense to develop a new, highly-capable, and relatively inexpensive fighter jet. It aimed to surpass existing jet designs in speed, maneuverability, and overall combat effectiveness. However, due to escalating budget constraints and shifting strategic priorities, the project was ultimately scaled back and abandoned in 1982, with significant portions of the research and development team reassigned to other defense initiatives.  It was deemed a costly and ultimately less effective endeavor compared to initial projections.
